title: Design Docs & Technical Planning
description: Writing effective design docs for Python projects at FAANG: templates, review process, tradeoff analysis, migration planning, and rollout strategies.

sections.title: 1. Why Write a Design Doc?
sections.description:
A design doc saves more time than it costs. Writing takes 2-8 hours. It prevents building the wrong thing for weeks or months.

When to write one: (1) Any project taking >1 week. (2) Cross-team changes. (3) Architectural decisions (database choice, framework, migration). (4) Public API design. (5) Any change that's hard to undo.

When NOT to write one: (1) Bug fixes. (2) Minor refactors. (3) Internal-only changes to a single component. (4) Experiments that will be thrown away.

FAANG angle: 'Your team wants to migrate from Flask to FastAPI. What do you do first?' — Write a design doc. Compare migration effort, performance gains, learning curve, and rollback plan. Without a design doc, the decision is based on vibes, not data.
sections.codeBlock:
# Decision matrix: Flask -> FastAPI migration
#
# Criteria            Weight   Flask  FastAPI  Notes
# Perf (req/s)        0.30     5/10   9/10     FastAPI 2x throughput (async)
# Migration effort    0.25     10/10  6/10     ~2 weeks per service
# Ecosystem           0.20     9/10   7/10     Flask has more extensions
# Team familiarity    0.15     9/10   5/10     3-month ramp-up
# Async support       0.10     3/10   10/10    Critical for new features
#
# Weighted score:
# Flask:  (0.30*5)+(0.25*10)+(0.20*9)+(0.15*9)+(0.10*3) = 7.1
# FastAPI: (0.30*9)+(0.25*6)+(0.20*7)+(0.15*5)+(0.10*10) = 7.3
#
# Verdict: FastAPI scores slightly higher; proceed with pilot service

sections.title: 2. Design Doc Template — the Google Standard
sections.description:
A design doc has 7 sections. Be concise — if it's >5 pages, you haven't scoped the problem tightly enough.

1. Context: Current state, the problem, why now. 2-3 paragraphs.
2. Goals and Non-Goals: What we ARE doing (measurable) and what we are NOT doing (scoping).
3. Options: 2-4 approaches with tradeoff analysis. Include 'do nothing' as an option.
4. Design: The chosen approach. Architecture diagram, data flow, API surface. 1-2 pages.
5. Rollout: Phased migration plan with rollback criteria at each phase.
6. Success Metrics: Quantified KPIs. 'Reduce p99 latency by 30%.'
7. Risks: Known unknowns, mitigation strategies.

FAANG Staff+ angle: A design doc that cuts scope aggressively is a signal of Staff-level judgment. 'We're NOT building a real-time dashboard in phase 1' is as important as what you ARE building.
sections.codeBlock:
# Design Doc Template (copy-paste ready)
# ======================================
# Title: [Project Name]
# Status: [Draft | Reviewing | Approved | Superseded]
# Author: [Name]
# Date: [Date]
# Reviewers: [Names]
#
# 1. Context
#    [Current state, problem statement, urgency]
#
# 2. Goals & Non-Goals
#    Goals:
#    - [Specific, measurable outcome 1]
#    - [Specific, measurable outcome 2]
#    Non-Goals:
#    - [Explicitly excluded scope]
#
# 3. Options
#    Option A: [Name] - [Brief desc] - [Pros/Cons/Effort]
#    Option B: [Name] - [Brief desc] - [Pros/Cons/Effort]
#    Option C: [Name] - [Brief desc] - [Pros/Cons/Effort]
#
# 4. Design
#    [Chosen architecture, data flow, API, diagram ref]
#
# 5. Rollout Plan
#    Phase 1: [Pilot, limited scope, success criteria, rollback]
#    Phase 2: [25% traffic, monitoring, success criteria]
#    Phase 3: [100% traffic, full rollout]
#
# 6. Success Metrics
#    - [Metric]: current=[X], target=[Y]
#
# 7. Risks
#    - [Risk]: [Likelihood], [Impact], [Mitigation]

sections.title: 3. Tradeoff Analysis — Structured Decision Making
sections.description:
Every engineering decision is a tradeoff. The design doc makes tradeoffs explicit so the team makes informed choices.

Common axes: (1) Build vs buy. (2) Consistency vs availability (CAP theorem). (3) Latency vs throughput. (4) Development speed vs reliability. (5) Monolith vs microservices.

Decision framework: (1) List criteria relevant to the problem. (2) Weight each criterion (0.0-1.0, sum to 1.0). (3) Score each option per criterion (1-10). (4) Compute weighted total. (5) Document the runner-up's strengths for future reference.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Why did we choose PostgreSQL over Spanner for this service?' — The design doc shows the tradeoff. We needed strong consistency within a region but < 10ms writes. Spanner's 50-100ms write latency was unacceptable. If consistency needs become global, we have a migration path documented.
sections.codeBlock:
# Tradeoff matrix template

# Decision: Database for user profiles service

# Criteria                          Weight  Postgres  Spanner  DynamoDB
# Read latency (p99 <20ms)           0.25    9         6        9
# Write latency (p99 <50ms)          0.20    8         4        9
# Strong consistency (within region) 0.20    10        10       5
# Global consistency (future need)   0.10    2         10       3
# Operational complexity             0.15    8         5        9
# Team familiarity                   0.10    9         3        7
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# Weighted score                     1.00    7.85      6.15     7.50

# Verdict: Postgres wins for current needs.
# Spanner if global consistency becomes a requirement.

# Key tradeoff: DynamoDB scores well but eventual consistency
# would require application-level conflict resolution.
# The 2-week delay in Postgres to Spanner migration is acceptable.

sections.title: 4. Migration Planning — Phased Rollouts and Rollbacks
sections.description:
Every migration must be reversible. If you can't roll back, you haven't planned the migration.

Phased strategy: (1) Dark launch (run the new system in parallel, compare outputs). (2) Canary (1% of traffic). (3) 10% -> 25% -> 50% -> 100%. Each phase has explicit success criteria and a rollback trigger.

Rollback criteria: Latency increases >20%. Error rate above baseline. P90 latency spike. Any P0/P1 incident in the component.

Data migration: Backward-compatible schema changes only (add columns, don't remove). Dual-write to old and new during transition. Backfill script for historical data.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Design the migration from a monolith to microservices' — Strangler Fig pattern: extract one service at a time, maintain backward compatibility, route traffic via API gateway, roll back per service independently.
sections.codeBlock:
# Migration plan: Monolith -> Microservices
#
# Phase 0: Extract shared library (models, utils) from monolith
#   - Goal: No behavior change, just structural
#   - Success: All tests pass
#   - Rollback: Revert import path changes
#
# Phase 1: Extract User Service (dark launch)
#   - Run User Service in parallel with monolith
#   - Compare outputs for 1 week
#   - Success: 100% output match
#   - Rollback: Point router back to monolith
#
# Phase 2: Route 1% of user traffic to User Service
#   - Monitor p50/p99 latency, error rate, saturation
#   - Success: <5% increase in p99 latency
#   - Rollback: Immediate traffic shift back (1 CLI command)
#
# Phase 3: Scale to 100% over 2 weeks
#   - 10% -> 25% -> 50% -> 100%
#   - Each step: observe for 24-48 hours
#   - Rollback at any step: redirect traffic to monolith
#
# Phase 4: Decommission user endpoints from monolith
#   - After 2 weeks of 100% stability
#   - Remove dead code, update tests

# Rollback script concept
# kubectl set image deployment/user-service user-service=monolith:v1.0

sections.title: 5. Staff+ Patterns — Technical Vision and Org-Level Planning
sections.description:
At Staff+ level, you write design docs that span multiple teams and quarters. Your technical vision influences the org's direction.

Technical vision doc: A 6-12 month forward-looking document. Different from a design doc — it identifies trends, unmet needs, and strategic investments. 'We should invest in Python 3.12 migration now because performance improvements and security patches for 3.8 end in Q3.'

Review process: (1) Share with stakeholders for early feedback. (2) Schedule a 30-min review meeting. (3) Address all comments within 1 week. (4) Revise and publish. (5) Archive in the team's doc repo.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'How do you get buy-in for a 6-month infrastructure project?' — Write a technical vision doc that: (1) Explains the current pain with data. (2) Projects future pain if not addressed. (3) Proposes a phased approach with early wins. (4) Shows how it enables faster feature development. (5) Gets sign-off from stakeholders.
sections.codeBlock:
# Technical Vision: Python Ecosystem at Acme (2026 H2)
#
# Current state:
# - 25 Python services, mix of 3.8-3.10
# - No type annotations in legacy services
# - CI pipeline averages 15 minutes
# - 2 Python readability reviewers for 50 engineers
#
# Vision:
# 'By Q1 2027, all Python services are on 3.12, fully typed,
#  with < 5 min CI and a Python readability culture.'
#
# Phases:
# Q3 2026: Python 3.12 migration (12 critical services)
#          Pilot mypy --strict on 1 service
#          Train 3 new readability reviewers
#
# Q4 2026: Complete 3.12 migration (all 25 services)
#          Enable mypy --strict on 5 services
#          Publish team Python style guide
#
# Q1 2027: mypy --strict on all production code
#          CI time < 5 min (parallel builds, caching)
#          10 readability reviewers (5 L4, 3 L5, 2 L6)
#
# Metrics:
# - Type coverage: 0% -> 100%
# - CI time: 15min -> 5min
# - Readability reviewers: 2 -> 10
# - Production bugs: -30%

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Design Docs at Google — Malte Schwarzkopf
  • The Staff Engineer's Path by Tanya Reilly — Chapter 4: Writing Design Docs
  • An Elegant Puzzle by Will Larson — Chapter 3: Organizational Design
  • Software Engineering at Google by Winters, Manshreck, Wright

details:
Design doc workflow: (1) Write a 1-2 page doc before writing code. (2) Get review from 2-3 engineers (at least one Staff+). (3) Be explicit about tradeoffs — document both the chosen AND rejected options. (4) Plan phased rollouts with rollback criteria. (5) After the project, update the doc with what actually happened (or just ship, and the code is the truth).

Common mistakes: Writing a design doc for everything (slows down simple changes); not including non-goals (scope creep); skipping the rollback plan (assumption of success); choosing an option without data (preference-driven decisions); not getting early stakeholder feedback.

Staff+ expectations: Write design docs that serve as reference documents for the org. Make tradeoff analysis data-driven. Anticipate future needs without over-engineering the present. Review others' design docs to improve their quality and scope management.
